functions.name: inspire_utils.dedupers.dedupe_list
documentation:
Remove duplicates from a list preserving the order.

We might be tempted to use the list(set(l)) idiom, but it doesn't
preserve the order, which hinders testability.

functions.name: inspire_utils.dedupers.dedupe_list_of_dicts
documentation:
Remove duplicates from a list of dictionaries preserving the order.

We can't use the generic list helper because a dictionary isn't
hashable. Adapted from
http://stackoverflow.com/a/9427216/374865.

functions.name: inspire_utils.dedupers.dedupe_all_lists
documentation:
Recursively remove duplicates from all lists.

Args:
    obj: collection to deduplicate
    exclude_keys (Container[str]): key names to ignore for deduplication
    dedupe_top_level (bool): whether the top-level list should be deduplicated too

functions.name: inspire_utils.query.wrap_queries_in_bool_clauses_if_more_than_one
documentation:
Helper for wrapping a list of queries into a bool.{must, should} clause.

Args:
    queries (list): List of queries to be wrapped in a bool.{must, should} clause.
    use_must_clause (bool): Flag that signifies whether to use
    'must' or 'should' clause.
    preserve_bool_semantics_if_one_clause (bool): Flag that signifies whether to
    generate a bool query even if there's only one clause. This happens to generate
    boolean query semantics. Usually not the case, but useful for boolean queries support.
Returns:
    (dict): If len(queries) > 1, the bool clause, otherwise if len(queries) == 1,
    will return the query itself,while finally, if len(queries) == 0,
    then an empty dictionary is returned.

functions.name: inspire_utils.query.ordered
documentation: Helper to order the dictionary.

functions.name: inspire_utils.helpers.force_list
documentation:
Force `data` to become a list.

You should use this method whenever you don't want to deal with the
fact that `NoneType` can't be iterated over. For example, instead
of writing:

```
bar = foo.get('bar')
if bar is not None:
    for el in bar:
        ...
```

you can write:

```
for el in force_list(foo.get('bar')):
    ...
```

Args:

: data: any Python object.

Returns:

: list: a list representation of `data`.

Examples:
: ```pycon
  >>> force_list(None)
  []
  >>> force_list('foo')
  ['foo']
  >>> force_list(('foo', 'bar'))
  ['foo', 'bar']
  >>> force_list(['foo', 'bar', 'baz'])
  ['foo', 'bar', 'baz']
  ```

functions.name: inspire_utils.helpers.maybe_float
documentation:
Return a `float` if possible, otherwise `None`.

Args:

: el: any Python object.

Returns:

: float: a `float` parsed from the object, or `None`.

Examples:
: ```pycon
  >>> maybe_float('35.0499505')
  35.0499505
  ```

functions.name: inspire_utils.helpers.maybe_int
documentation:
Return an `int` if possible, otherwise `None`.

Args:

: el: any Python object.

Returns:

: Union\[int, NoneType\]: an `int` parsed from the object, or `None`.

Examples:
: ```pycon
  >>> maybe_int('10')
  10
  ```

functions.name: inspire_utils.helpers.remove_tags
documentation:
Selectively remove tags. This removes all tags in `dirty`, stripping also the contents of tags matching the XPath selector in `strip`, and keeping all tags that are subtags of tags in `allowed_trees` and tags in `allowed_tags`. Args: : dirty(Union[str, scrapy.selector.Selector, lxml.etree.\_Element]): the : input to clean up. allowed_tags(Container): tags to be kept in the output, but not necessarily : its subtags. allowed_trees(Container): tags to be kept, along with all its subtags, : in the output. strip(str): optional xpath selector. If it matches a tag, its : contents will also be stripped. Useful axes are `@` for attribute access and `self` to select a given tag. Returns: : str: the textual content of `dirty`, with some tags kept and some text removed. Examples: : ```pycon >>> tag = '

Only this text remains. Not this one.

' >>> remove_tags(tag, allowed_tree=('b',), strip='@class="hidden"') u'Only this text remains.' >>> remove_tags(tag, allowed_tags=('b',), strip='@class="hidden"') u'Only this text remains.' >>> remove_tags(tag, allowed_tags=('b',), strip='self::span') u'Only this text remains.' ```

functions.name: inspire_utils.urls.ensure_scheme
documentation:
Adds a scheme to a url if not present.

Args:
    url (string): a url, assumed to start with netloc
    default_scheme (string): a scheme to be added

Returns:
    string: URL with a scheme

functions.name: inspire_utils.urls.record_url_by_pattern
documentation:
Get a URL to a record constructing it from a pattern.

Args:
    pattern (string): a URL pattern as a format-friendly string with a
        `recid` field
    recid (Union[string, int]): record ID

Returns:
    string: built record URL

functions.name: inspire_utils.record.get_value
documentation:
Return item as `dict.__getitem__` but using 'smart queries'.

:::{note}
Accessing one value in a normal way, meaning d['a'], is almost as
fast as accessing a regular dictionary. But using the special
name convention is a bit slower than using the regular access:
.. code-block:: python

> ```pycon
> >>> %timeit x = dd['a[0].b']
> 100000 loops, best of 3: 3.94 us per loop
> >>> %timeit x = dd['a'][0]['b']
> 1000000 loops, best of 3: 598 ns per loop
> ```
:::

functions.name: inspire_utils.record.get_values_for_schema
documentation:
Return all values from elements having a given schema.

Args:

: elements(Iterable[dict]): an iterable of elements, which are all dicts

  : having at least the `schema` and `value` keys.

  schema(str): the schema that the values need to follow.

Returns:

: list: all values conforming to the given schema.

Example:
: ```pycon
  >>> elements = [
  ...     {'schema': 'TWITTER', 'value': 's_w_hawking'},
  ...     {'schema': 'WIKIPEDIA', 'value': 'Stephen_Hawking'}
  ... ]
  >>> get_values_for_schema(elements, 'TWITTER')
  ['s_w_hawking']
  ```

functions.name: inspire_utils.record.replace_undesirable_characters
documentation:
Replace certain bad characters in a text line. @param line: (string) the
text line in which bad characters are to.

be replaced. @return: (string) the text line after the
bad characters have been                   replaced.

functions.name: inspire_utils.date.normalize_date
documentation:
Normalize a date to the be schema-compliant.

This is a convenience wrapper around {ref}`PartialDate`, which should be
used instead if more features are needed.

Note:

: When `date` is `None` this returns `None` instead of raising
  an exception because this makes `DoJSON`'s code simpler, as it
  already knows how to strip `None` values at the end.

Args:

: date(str): date to normalize
  kwargs: these are passed to the `dateutil.parser.parse` function

  > that is used internally to parse the date. Most notably, the
  > `yearfirst` and `datefirst` flags can be used if the ordering
  > of the date parts is know.

Returns:
: str: normalized date, in the form `YYYY-MM-DD`, `YYYY-MM` or

  : `YYYY` (depending on the information present in the date).

Raises:

: ValueError: when the date cannot be parsed or no year is present.

Examples:
: ```pycon
  >>> normalize_date(None)
  >>> normalize_date('30 Jun 1686')
  '1686-06-30'
  ```

functions.name: inspire_utils.date.format_date
documentation:
Format a schema-compliant date string in a human-friendy format.

This is a convenience wrapper around {ref}`PartialDate`, which should be
used instead if more features are needed.

functions.name: inspire_utils.date.earliest_date
documentation:
Return the earliest among the schema-compliant dates.

This is a convenience wrapper around {ref}`PartialDate`, which should be
used instead if more features are needed.

Args:

: dates(list): List of dates from which oldest/earliest one will be returned

Returns:

: str: Earliest date from provided list

functions.name: inspire_utils.date.fill_missing_date_parts
documentation:
Sets missing day and/or month to 1.

Useful to avoid errors when saving to DB.

classes.name: inspire_utils.date.PartialDate

methods.name: inspire_utils.date.PartialDate.loads
documentation:
Load a date from a string in a record.

This can also be used to validate a date.

Examples:
    >>> PartialDate.loads('1686-06')
    PartialDate(year=1686, month=6, day=None)
    >>> PartialDate.loads('1686-42')
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
    ValueError: month must be in 1..12

methods.name: inspire_utils.date.PartialDate.dumps
documentation:
Dump the date for serialization into the record.

Returns:
: str: normalized date, in the form `YYYY-MM-DD`, `YYYY-MM` or

  : `YYYY` (depending on the information present in the date)

methods.name: inspire_utils.date.PartialDate.parse
documentation:
Parse a date given in arbitrary format.

This attempts to parse the input date, given in an arbitrary format

Args:
    date(str): date to normalize
    **kwargs: these are passed to the `dateutil.parser.parse` function
        which is used internally to parse the date. Most notably, the
        `yearfirst` and `datefirst` flags can be used if the ordering
        of the date parts is known.

Returns:
    PartialDate: an object holding the parsed date.

Raises:
    ValueError: when the date cannot be parsed or no year is present.

Examples:
    >>> PartialDate.parse('30 Jun 1686')
    PartialDate(year=1686, month=6, day=30)

methods.name: inspire_utils.date.PartialDate.from_parts
documentation:
Build a PartialDate from its parts.

Unlike the standard constructor, the parts don't have to be `int` s but
can be strings containing textual month information.

Examples:
    >>> PartialDate.from_parts('1686', 'June', '30')
    PartialDate(year=1686, month=6, day=30)

methods.name: inspire_utils.date.PartialDate.pprint
documentation:
Pretty print the date.

Examples:
    >>> PartialDate(1686, 6, 30).pprint()
    u'Jun 30, 1686'

Class for representing a partial date.

The standard constructor assumes that all date parts are known (or not
present) and have already been converted to `int` s. For more flexibility,
see {ref}`PartialDate.from_parts` and {ref}`PartialDate.parse`.

Two `PartialDate` s can be compared and a more complete date is considered
smaller than the same date with parts removed.

Raises:

: TypeError: when the date parts are not `int` s or `None`.
  ValueError: when the date is not valid.

functions.name: inspire_utils.name._prepare_nameparser_constants
documentation:
Prepare nameparser Constants.

Remove nameparser's titles and use our own and add as suffixes the
roman numerals. Configuration is the same for all names (i.e.
instances).

functions.name: inspire_utils.name.normalize_name
documentation:
Normalize name.

Args:
    name (six.text_type): The name to be normalized.

Returns:
    str: The normalized name.

functions.name: inspire_utils.name._generate_non_lastnames_variations
documentation:
Generate variations for all non-lastnames.

E.g. For 'John Richard', this method generates: [     'John', 'J',
'Richard', 'R', 'John Richard', 'John R', 'J Richard', 'J R', ]

functions.name: inspire_utils.name._generate_lastnames_variations
documentation:
Generate variations for lastnames.

Note:
    This method follows the assumption that the first last name is the main one.
    E.g. For 'Caro Estevez', this method generates: ['Caro', 'Caro Estevez'].
    In the case the lastnames are dashed, it splits them in two.

functions.name: inspire_utils.name.generate_name_variations
documentation:
Generate name variations for a given name.

Args:
    name (six.text_type): The name whose variations are to be generated.

Returns:
    list: All the name variations for the given name.

Notes:
    Uses `unidecode` for doing unicode characters transliteration to ASCII ones.
    This was chosen so that we can map both full names of authors in HEP records
    and user's input to the same space and thus make exact queries work.

functions.name: inspire_utils.name.format_name
documentation:
Format a schema-compliant name string in a human-friendy format.

This is a convenience wrapper around {ref}`ParsedName`, which should be
used instead if more features are needed.

Args:

: name (str): The name to format, in pretty much any format.
  initials_only (bool): `True` if we want the first names to be

  > displayed with only the initial followed by a dot. `False` otherwise.

Examples:
\>>> format_name('Lieber, Stanley Martin')
u'Stanley Martin Lieber'
\>>> format_name('Lieber, Stanley Martin', initials_only=True)
u'S. M. Lieber'
\>>> format_name('Downey, Robert Jr.', initials_only=True)
u'R. Downey Jr.'

variables.name: inspire_utils.name._NAMES_MAX_NUMBER_THRESHOLD
documentation:
Threshold for skipping the combinatorial expansion of names (when generating
name variations).

classes.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName

methods.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName.__init__
documentation:
Create a ParsedName instance.

Args:

: name (Union[str, HumanName]): The name to be parsed (must be non empty nor None).
  constants ({class}`nameparser.config.Constants`): Configuration

  > for `HumanName` instantiation.
  > (Can be None, if provided it overwrites the default one generated in
  > {method}`prepare_nameparser_constants`.)

methods.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName.loads
documentation:
Load a parsed name from a string.

Raises:
    TypeError: when name isn't a type of `six.string_types`.
    ValueError: when name is empty or None.

methods.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName.dumps
documentation: Dump the name to string, after normalizing it.

methods.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName.pprint
documentation:
Pretty print the name.

Args:

: initials_only (bool): `True` if we want the first names to be displayed with
  only the initial followed by a dot. `False` otherwise.

Examples:
: ```pycon
  >>> ParsedName('Lieber, Stanley Martin').pprint()
  u'Stanley Martin Lieber'
  >>> ParsedName('Lieber, Stanley Martin').pprint(initials_only=True)
  u'S. M. Lieber'
  >>> ParsedName('Downey, Robert Jr.').pprint(initials_only=True)
  u'R. Downey Jr.'
  ```

methods.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName.generate_es_query
documentation:
Generates a query handling specifically authors.

Notes:

: There are three main cases:
  1\) `a Smith`
  This will just generate a `match` query on `last_name`
  2\) `a John Smith`

  > This will just generate a `match` query on `last_name` and a `prefix`
  > query on `first_name` and a `match` query on the initial `J`.
  > This will return results from `Smith, John` and `Smith, J`
  > but not from `Smith, Jane`.

  3\) `a J Smith`
  This will just generate a `match` query on `last_name` and a match
  query on `first_name.initials`.
  Please note, cases such as `J.D.` have been properly handled by the tokenizer.

class_variables.name: inspire_utils.name.ParsedName.constants
documentation:
The default constants configuration for `HumanName` to use for parsing
all names.

Class for representing a name.

After construction, the instance exposes the fields exposed by `HumanName` instance, i.e.
`title`, `first`, `middle`, `last`, `suffix`.
